summary: Spolsky argues that upfront design is essential in software development because nothing is ever as simple as it seems. He illustrates this with a CityDesk usability fix where three minutes of design work on paper saved hours of coding by simplifying a wizard into two menu items. He pushes back against the perception that Extreme Programming advocates zero design, arguing that most programmers use 'no design' as an excuse for laziness that ultimately costs more time. He acknowledges that massive systems must evolve organically, but insists that feature-level design before implementation is non-negotiable for reducing schedule risk and building better software.
key_points: A simple usability fix turned complex when Spolsky almost built an unnecessary wizard — writing a brief spec first revealed a much simpler two-menu-item solution
Nothing in software is as simple as it seems: even basic operations like file copying have dozens of edge cases and failure modes
Schedule risk is dangerous because decisions made assuming short timelines become wrong when features take 20x longer than expected
Extreme Programming doesn't actually advocate zero design, but programmers hear what they want to hear and use it as an excuse to skip planning
Linus Torvalds' skepticism of upfront design applies to massive evolving systems, not to everyday feature development — and he's smarter than most of us anyway
money_quotes: Nothing is as simple as it seems.
Three minutes of design work saved me hours of coding.
You have to design things before you implement them.
You can put wheels on your mama but that doesn't make her a bus.
It's one of those weird forms of laziness where you end up doing more work than you would have done otherwise.
Most programmers are looking for any excuse they can find not to do basic design before implementing features. So they latch onto the 'no-design' idea like flies in a bug zapper.
Linus is much smarter than we are, so things that work for him don't work for us normal people.
When you sit down to write File Copy, or when you sit down to plan the features of the next release of your software, you gotta design. Don't let the sirens persuade you otherwise.
key_insight: A few minutes spent designing on paper before coding consistently saves hours of wasted implementation work, because nothing in software is ever as simple as it first appears.
